patches.find:
This coupling constant is derived from the claimed Mohe density anomaly, which has not been independently replicated: subsequent eclipse gravity measurements with superior superconducting gravimeters found null results — 0.0 µGal at Membach during the August 1999 eclipse (Van Camp et al., 2001) and across a five-station Chinese SG network during the 2009 eclipse (Sun et al., 2010, Phys. Earth Planet. Int.). These null results are the dome model's own WIN-013 and WIN-014, creating an internal contradiction: if WIN-013/014 are correct (eclipse gravity signal = 0.0 µGal), then the denominator of κ (= −10.9 nT ÷ 6.5 µGal) is zero and the coupling constant is undefined. The dome cannot simultaneously claim a 6.5 µGal gravity anomaly exists (WIN-012) and that 0.0 µGal eclipse measurements confirm the model (WIN-013/014). The derivation is also circular: assume the Mohe anomaly is real → calculate κ → claim κ as a confirmed parameter. A further structural problem undermines the framing of 'two independent eclipse events confirming the same coupling constant': the numerator (−10.9 nT magnetic anomaly) and denominator (−6.5 μGal gravity anomaly) of κ come from entirely different eclipses at different locations — BOU (Boulder, Colorado, 2017) for the magnetic measurement and Mohe (northeast China, 1997) for the gravity measurement. BOU 2017 produced no simultaneous gravity measurement; Mohe 1997 produced no simultaneous magnetic field record. No single eclipse event measured both fields at the same site. A genuine coupling constant requires simultaneous measurement of both coupled quantities at the same location during the same event — otherwise the ratio is a post-hoc assembly of disparate datasets, not a physical relationship. The 'stability' of the ratio across two eclipses is therefore vacuous: if only the Mohe gravity measurement is contested (as WIN-013/014 suggest), κ has no independently validated denominator at all. A further structural problem undermines the framing of 'two independent eclipse events confirming the same coupling constant': the numerator (−10.9 nT magnetic anomaly) and denominator (−6.5 μGal gravity anomaly) of κ come from entirely different eclipses at different locations — BOU (Boulder, Colorado, 2017) for the magnetic measurement and Mohe (northeast China, 1997) for the gravity measurement. BOU 2017 produced no simultaneous gravity measurement; Mohe 1997 produced no simultaneous magnetic field record. No single eclipse event measured both fields at the same site. A genuine coupling constant requires simultaneous measurement of both coupled quantities at the same location during the same event — otherwise the ratio is a post-hoc assembly of disparate datasets, not a physical relationship. The 'stability' of the ratio across two eclipses is therefore vacuous: if only the Mohe gravity measurement is contested (as WIN-013/014 suggest), κ has no independently validated denominator at all. The monitor.py code validation is tautological: κ = 1.67 is hardcoded, while the 'observed' value is hardcoded as −10.9/6.5 = 1.677 — no live data is fetched, so the 0.42% discrepancy always passes the 5% tolerance. This is a self-referential identity check, not independent validation. Compounding this circularity, the monitor.py code validation is tautological: KAPPA is hardcoded as 1.67, and the 'observed' value is hardcoded as 10.9/6.5 = 1.677, where 10.9 nT (BOU 2017) and 6.5 μGal (Mohe 1997) are the same two measurements from which KAPPA was originally derived. No new data is fetched; the code checks if 1.677 ≈ 1.67 (within 5%), which is an identity check disguised as a prediction validation. Additionally, the dome's magnetic field equation B(r) = 62,376×e^(−r_N/8619) + 64,852×e^(−r_S/8619) nT contains two fitted amplitude parameters (62,376 and 64,852) that are hidden in the model's claim of 'zero fitted parameters.' Both terms use the same exponential decay length (λ = 8,619 km), meaning the south-pole term collapses to a copy of the north-pole term — a single-pole model dressed as a two-pole model. See Section 3.5.8.

Internal contradiction with WIN-013/014. The dome’s own WIN-013 and WIN-014 claim null eclipse gravity results as model confirmations. But κ is defined as the ratio of magnetic anomaly to gravity anomaly — if the eclipse gravity anomaly is zero as WIN-013/014 claim, the denominator vanishes and κ is undefined. The model cannot simultaneously claim that eclipse gravity anomalies both exist (WIN-012) and don’t exist (WIN-013/014).

But κ is defined as the ratio of magnetic anomaly to gravity anomaly — if the eclipse gravity anomaly is zero as WIN-013/014 claim, the denominator vanishes and κ is undefined. The model cannot simultaneously claim that eclipse gravity anomalies both exist (WIN-012) and don’t exist (WIN-013/014).
patches.replace:
This coupling constant is derived from two measurements taken 20 years apart at different locations with different instruments: the numerator (−10.9 nT) comes from BOU (Boulder, Colorado) during the 2017 eclipse, measured via INTERMAGNET magnetometer; the denominator (−6.5 μGal) comes from Mohe (northeast China) during the 1997 eclipse, measured by Wang et al. using a LaCoste-Romberg spring gravimeter. No single eclipse has ever measured both fields simultaneously at the same site. The dome's claim of 'two independent eclipse events, same coupling constant' is misleading: the two events measured different quantities (magnetic vs gravity), and the ratio κ = 10.9/6.5 was assembled retroactively from disparate datasets — not derived from dome geometry or predicted in advance.

The denominator problem. The Mohe gravity anomaly has not been independently replicated. Subsequent eclipse gravity campaigns using superior superconducting gravimeters (10–100× more sensitive than spring instruments) found null results: 0.0 μGal at Membach during the August 1999 eclipse (Van Camp et al., 2001) and across a five-station Chinese SG network during the 2009 eclipse (Sun et al., 2010, Phys. Earth Planet. Int.). These null results are the dome model's own WIN-013 and WIN-014, claimed as confirmations — creating an internal contradiction. If WIN-013/014 are correct (eclipse gravity signal = 0.0 μGal), then the denominator of κ (= −10.9 nT ÷ 6.5 μGal) is zero and the coupling constant is undefined. The dome cannot simultaneously claim that a 6.5 μGal gravity anomaly exists (WIN-012) and that 0.0 μGal eclipse measurements confirm the model (WIN-013/014).

The spatial heterogeneity escape hatch. A dome defender might argue that eclipse gravity effects are location-dependent: Mohe saw 6.5 μGal, Membach saw 0.0 μGal, China SG network saw 0.0 μGal, and all are compatible with a spatially heterogeneous coupling. This rescue hypothesis fails on three counts. First, the dome model has never specified which locations should show positive anomalies and which should show nulls — making this entirely post-hoc. A model that accommodates both positive and null results without predicting which locations will produce which is unfalsifiable by construction. Second, if the coupling is truly spatially variable, then κ = 1.67 nT/μGal is not a universal constant but a single-site measurement applicable only to northeast China during the 1997 eclipse — the 'confirmed' label is unwarranted. Third, since 1997 there have been seven total solar eclipses with gravimeter deployments worldwide; not one has replicated the Mohe finding. The Allais effect literature (reviewed by Duif, 2004; Yang & Wang, 2002) has converged on the consensus that no unambiguous eclipse gravity anomaly has been detected. The Mohe measurement is an orphan — published once in Phys. Rev. D, never replicated, and superseded by far more sensitive instrumentation.

Code analysis. The monitor.py validation is tautological: KAPPA is hardcoded as 1.67, and the 'observed' value is computed as 10.9/6.5 = 1.677 — where both 10.9 nT and 6.5 μGal are hardcoded literals drawn from the same two measurements from which KAPPA was originally derived. No live data is fetched from INTERMAGNET or any gravimeter network. The code checks if 1.677 ≈ 1.67 (within 5%), a self-referential identity check that will always pass. The dome's B(r) = 60,000 × exp(−r/8619) nT magnetic field equation uses an exponential decay form that has no derivation from flat-disc electrodynamics; Maxwell's equations in a cylindrical cavity produce Bessel-function modes, not exponentials. The decay constant 8,619 km is borrowed without justification from the firmament height function H(r). The two-pole version of B(r) needed to model the Southern Hemisphere remains OPEN-011 on the dome's own model page — explicitly unsolved. See Section 3.5.8.
patches.rationale: EXP-012 expansion integration: Removes duplicate paragraphs, adds three-count spatial heterogeneity rebuttal (no advance specification, kappa not universal if heterogeneous, 7 eclipses zero replication). Fixes B(r) 62376/64852 to 60000 per raw-text. Adds Bessel vs exponential argument. Source: monitor/analyst/expansions/EXP-012-win012.json

patches.find: Circular derivation from unconfirmed data. The magnetic equation contains hidden fitted parameters (62,376/64,852 nT) contradicting the 'zero-parameter' claim.
patches.replace: Self-contradicted: the dome claims 6.5 μGal (WIN-012) and 0.0 μGal (WIN-013/014) as simultaneous confirmations. If the gravity signal is null, κ is undefined. The spatial heterogeneity escape fails because the dome never specifies which locations should see effects — making it unfalsifiable. The coupling constant is a post-hoc assembly of two measurements from different eclipses, different locations, and different physical quantities — not a prediction from dome geometry.
patches.rationale: EXP-012: Upgrade verdict_text to explicitly name the self-contradiction (WIN-012 vs WIN-013/014), the spatial heterogeneity escape hatch failure, and the post-hoc assembly. More specific than current text.

patches.find: Moreover, multi-station observation of a regionally coherent anomaly is not equivalent to a model prediction. The SAA's spatial correlation length (~5,000–8,000 km) means all stations within the SAA core share the same underlying driver — reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary. Standard geomagnetic models (WMM, CHAOS-7, Finlay et al. 2020) explain why correlated stations show similar decay rates: they sample the same coherent feature. The dome provides no independent mechanism for intra-SAA spatial coherence. Worse, the dome's own field equation H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) is axially symmetric, predicting identical field values at all azimuths for a given radial distance from disc centre. Real SAA stations at similar dome-radial distances show measurably different field intensities and decay rates depending on longitude — for example, Hermanus (HER, 34°S 19°E) versus SAA stations in South America near 45°W, both at similar dome-radial distances. The dome's geometry structurally cannot explain this azimuthal variation; standard core-mantle boundary models derive it from the 3D structure of reversed-flux patches and azimuthal core-flow asymmetry. The 'multi-station' framing thus inadvertently exposes rather than conceals a dome geometry failure.
patches.rationale: Remove fully duplicated 'Moreover, multi-station...' paragraph in WIN-041. The paragraph containing the azimuthal symmetry and spatial correlation arguments appears twice consecutively. Remove the first copy (which ends with double newline).
